problems.title: The Accessibility Crisis
problems.briefDescription:
Your company, TechVision Learning, has just launched a new online learning platform designed to help students across the UK improve their digital skills. However, within weeks of launch, you've received complaints from users with disabilities—students using screen readers report that course materials are unusable, deaf students cannot access video content without captions, and students with motor impairments struggle with the navigation interface.

The situation has escalated: accessibility advocacy groups have begun a social media campaign criticizing TechVision Learning, and several schools have threatened to stop using your platform. The company's leadership team has called an emergency meeting and asked your technical team to investigate and fix these critical issues.

Your role is to lead the development decisions that will determine how quickly and effectively your team addresses these accessibility barriers. Every decision you make will impact your company's reputation (Trust), the inclusivity of your platform (Inclusivity), the speed of implementation (Impact), and your development budget (Budget in £).

You have multiple stages of critical decisions ahead. Your goal is to make choices that balance accessibility improvements with realistic constraints, while keeping your team and stakeholders satisfied. The stakes are high—this is about ensuring that all students, regardless of ability, can access quality education.
problems.objectives: Lead your team through accessibility crisis management. Make decisions that improve platform accessibility while managing team resources and company reputation.

stages.title: Phase 1: Rapid Accessibility Assessment
stages.description:
Your team needs to understand the full scope of accessibility issues before diving into fixes. You've received initial complaints about screen readers, captions, and keyboard navigation. You decide it's time to conduct a comprehensive audit. Two options are on the table:

Option A: Hire an external accessibility consultancy (cost: £8,000) to conduct a thorough 2-week audit using specialized testing tools and real users with disabilities. This will give you a complete picture of all issues and recommended fixes, building credibility with accessibility advocates.

Option B: Have your internal team conduct a self-assessment using free accessibility tools and industry guidelines (cost: £1,500 for tools). This is faster to start but may miss some issues and might not satisfy external critics who want independent verification.

choices.text: Hire external accessibility consultancy for comprehensive audit
Use internal team for self-assessment with free tools

stages.title: Phase 2: Building Your Accessibility Team
stages.description:
The assessment is complete, and the results are clear: your platform has significant accessibility gaps. Now you need to assemble a team to fix these issues. You need developers, designers, and someone with lived experience of disability to guide your decisions. You're considering two approaches:

Option A: Hire a dedicated accessibility specialist (£3,500/month) who has a visual impairment and years of experience testing with assistive technologies. This person will be essential for validating your fixes and advising on real-world usability.

Option B: Distribute accessibility responsibilities among existing team members (£500 extra per month for training courses). This saves money but your team is already stretched thin, and they lack expertise.

choices.text: Hire dedicated accessibility specialist
Train existing team to handle accessibility (lower cost)

stages.title: Phase 3: Implementation Strategy
stages.description:
With your team assembled, you need to decide how to tackle the fixes. Your backlog includes adding captions to 500+ videos, rewriting the navigation for keyboard access, improving color contrast, and making the interface work with screen readers. You're faced with a critical decision:

Option A: Launch a phased rollout over 3 months, making public commitments and updates every 2 weeks (£12,000). This builds trust through transparency and allows you to gather user feedback early, but delays full accessibility.

Option B: Work privately for 6 weeks and launch all fixes at once when complete (£9,000). This is faster overall but risks further criticism if people feel ignored during the wait.

choices.text: Phased rollout with public updates every 2 weeks
Complete all fixes privately, launch everything at once

stages.title: Phase 4: Community Engagement
stages.description:
As your team makes progress, you realize that the accessibility advocates and disabled users who originally complained need to be part of the solution. Ignoring them could undermine all your hard work. You're deciding whether to:

Option A: Form an accessibility advisory board (£2,000) with 8-10 disabled users who will test your platform monthly and provide feedback. This is more time-consuming but creates genuine partnerships and catches issues early.

Option B: Collect feedback through a simple online survey (£300). Quick and cheap, but less likely to inform you about nuanced issues that real users encounter.

choices.text: Create accessibility advisory board with real disabled users
Use simple online survey for feedback

stages.title: Phase 5: Long-Term Accessibility
stages.description:
Your fixes are nearly complete, and accessibility is improving dramatically. But you know from research that accessibility isn't a one-time project—it requires ongoing commitment. You're deciding how to ensure accessibility doesn't regress after this crisis is resolved:

Option A: Build accessibility testing into every release (£4,500 for automated tools + training). This prevents new accessibility issues in future updates and shows you're serious about long-term inclusion.

Option B: Say the crisis is resolved and move the accessibility specialist to other projects (saves £3,500/month). This frees up budget but risks creating new accessibility problems with future updates.

choices.text: Invest in permanent accessibility testing and practices
Consider accessibility crisis solved, reassign specialist

stages.title: Phase 6: Documentation and Knowledge Sharing
stages.description:
Your team has learned so much about accessibility. You now have a choice about how to capture and share this knowledge:

Option A: Create comprehensive accessibility documentation and publish case studies (£3,000) showing your journey. This helps other educators and tech companies learn from your experience, building your reputation as accessibility leaders.

Option B: Keep documentation internal only (£500). Focus on your own platform rather than helping competitors—you keep your competitive advantage but miss an opportunity to shift the industry.

choices.text: Publish comprehensive accessibility documentation and case studies
Keep documentation internal only

stages.title: Phase 7: Expanding to Mobile and Assistive Tech Support
stages.description:
Your desktop platform is now accessible, but 60% of your users access your platform on mobile phones. Many disabled students rely on mobile-specific assistive technologies. You're considering:

Option A: Fully redesign the app for mobile accessibility (£7,000). This ensures blind users can navigate via screen readers, deaf users get captions in videos, and motor-impaired users can use voice commands. Comprehensive but expensive.

Option B: Make minor mobile improvements (£2,000). Your app works on mobile but doesn't leverage mobile-specific accessibility features. Partially solves the problem.

choices.text: Fully redesign mobile app for complete accessibility
Make minor mobile usability improvements

problems.title: The Data Privacy Scandal
problems.theme: Student Data Protection and GDPR Compliance
problems.briefDescription:
It's Tuesday morning, and your team has just discovered something alarming. A security researcher contacted your company to report that student personal data—including names, email addresses, dates of birth, and learning progress records—is accessible through an unsecured API endpoint that you didn't know was public.

The data breach affects approximately 15,000 students from 25 schools across the UK. Under GDPR regulations, you have 72 hours to notify affected individuals, and there are potential fines of up to £20 million for serious violations.

Your company's executives are panicking. Parents are starting to call schools. The media is asking questions. Your team has been pulled into an emergency crisis meeting, and every decision you make in the next few hours will determine whether this becomes a minor incident or a catastrophic reputation disaster.

As the technical lead, you need to navigate the complex choices around transparency, remediation, resource allocation, and stakeholder communication. You have limited time and a limited budget to fix this security issue, manage the crisis, and rebuild trust.

Your decisions will affect how quickly you resolve the technical problem (Impact), whether affected students and families feel you're being honest (Trust), whether you protect vulnerable young people's futures (Inclusivity of care), and whether you have the resources to do all this properly (Budget in £).
problems.objectives: Navigate a data privacy crisis. Make decisions that secure student data, comply with regulations, and restore public trust while managing limited resources.

stages.title: Stage 1: Immediate Crisis Response
stages.description:
You have 72 hours to notify affected users and regulators. The security issue needs an immediate temporary fix, and you need to decide how transparent to be. Your options:

Option A: Immediately shut down the vulnerable API endpoint (£250), publish a detailed timeline of what happened on your website, and proactively notify affected families by email within 24 hours. This is honest but might intensify media scrutiny.

Option B: Quickly patch the endpoint with temporary access controls (£800), issue a brief statement saying you've fixed a "technical issue," and wait for regulators to contact you before providing details. Faster and less attention-grabbing, but feels evasive.

choices.text: Immediate shutdown + transparent notification to all affected families
Quick temporary patch + minimal public statement

stages.title: Stage 2: Investigation and Root Cause Analysis
stages.description:
You need to understand how this happened and whether any data was actually accessed by unauthorized people. You have two paths:

Option A: Hire external forensic cybersecurity firm (£6,500) to conduct a complete investigation, review server logs, and produce a detailed report. This is expensive but provides independent credibility and might identify other vulnerabilities.

Option B: Do an internal investigation (£1,200 for tools). Your team reviews logs themselves. It's cheaper but your conclusions might be questioned by parents and regulators.

choices.text: Hire external forensic cybersecurity firm for full investigation
Conduct internal investigation with minimal cost

stages.title: Stage 3: Remediation and Rebuilding
stages.description:
Whether or not data was actually stolen, you need to rebuild your security architecture. Two competing approaches:

Option A: Comprehensive security overhaul (£14,000): Implement a new API authentication system, encrypt all student data at rest, hire a full-time security engineer, and conduct a complete external penetration test. This takes 8 weeks but creates genuinely strong defenses.

Option B: Emergency quick-fix security patch (£4,000): Add access controls to the broken endpoint and implement basic encryption. Faster (2 weeks) but doesn't address deeper vulnerabilities.

choices.text: Comprehensive security overhaul with external testing
Quick emergency patch on vulnerable endpoint

stages.title: Stage 4: Student and Family Support
stages.description:
Affected families are worried, and several are considering legal action. You need to decide on your support approach:

Option A: Offer free one-year identity theft insurance (£3,200) to all affected students and their families, plus a dedicated support line (£1,500) staffed 9am-7pm weekdays by someone who can answer questions empathetically. This acknowledges the seriousness of the breach.

Option B: Provide a standard apology letter and point people to the UK government's advice on data breaches (£100). Does the minimum required.

choices.text: Offer identity theft insurance and dedicated support line
Standard apology and government resource links

stages.title: Stage 5: Future Governance and Transparency
stages.description:
To rebuild trust and prevent future crises, you need sustainable security governance:

Option A: Establish a privacy office (hire £2,800/month for a privacy officer) and create a public transparency report showing security metrics and privacy improvements quarterly. This signals serious commitment but requires ongoing investment.

Option B: Appoint an existing team member to oversee privacy (£300/month extra) and keep security details confidential. Much cheaper but less transparent.

choices.text: Establish dedicated privacy office with public transparency reports
Assign privacy role to existing team member

stages.title: Stage 6: Legal and Insurance Strategy
stages.description:
As the crisis unfolds, you're facing potential lawsuits from affected families and regulatory fines. You need a legal strategy:

Option A: Hire a specialist data protection lawyer (£5,000) to represent your company and negotiate with regulators. This maximizes your legal protection but is expensive and might look adversarial to families.

Option B: Work with your existing corporate counsel (£1,000). Less specialized but cheaper and shows you're not over-lawyering the situation.

choices.text: Hire specialist data protection lawyer for full legal representation
Work with existing corporate counsel

stages.title: Stage 7: Ongoing Communication and Rebuilding
stages.description:
The breach is stabilized, but families are still worried. You need to decide how to rebuild public trust:

Option A: Launch a comprehensive communication campaign (£4,200) with monthly town halls, detailed security updates sent to all schools, and a "data protection pledge" that you stand behind. Shows genuine commitment to change.

Option B: Issue periodic email updates (£500) and assume the crisis will fade. Minimal engagement but cheaper.

choices.text: Launch comprehensive communication campaign with regular town halls
Issue periodic email updates and move forward

problems.title: The UX Barrier Problem
problems.theme: Inclusive Interface Design for Diverse Learning Styles
problems.briefDescription:
Your educational platform was designed by a small team of experienced developers with a very specific vision: minimalist, clean, and fast. It won awards for its design.

But then something unexpected happened: teachers started reporting that many students struggled to use it. Shy students found the large empty spaces intimidating and didn't know where to click. Students with ADHD reported getting overwhelmed by options and freezing. Older learners without much tech experience felt lost. Neurodivergent students reported finding the aesthetic confusing rather than calming.

Usage data shows it clearly: while tech-savvy students loved your platform, a significant portion of your user base—potentially 40%—were struggling.

Your product team has concluded that the problem isn't that students are incapable. It's that your interface design is optimized for one type of learner while excluding many others. You need to make a difficult choice: do you redesign for inclusivity, and if so, how much does that cost?

Your decisions will impact how many students can effectively use your platform (Impact), how well you serve different learning styles (Inclusivity), whether you maintain your design reputation (Trust), and whether you have the budget to make these changes (Budget in £).
problems.objectives: Transform a beautiful but exclusive interface into one that welcomes all learners. Make design decisions that balance innovation with accessibility while managing team capacity and costs.

stages.title: Stage 1: Understanding the Problem
stages.description:
Before you can fix the design, you need to understand exactly who's struggling and why. You're choosing between two research approaches:

Option A: Conduct real user testing sessions (£5,000) where you observe 12-16 students of varying ages, learning styles, and tech experience actually using your platform and talk to them afterward. This takes 3 weeks but gives you deep, specific insights.

Option B: Use a quick anonymous survey (£400) asking students to rate different interface elements. Faster and cheaper but less detailed.

choices.text: Conduct detailed user testing with diverse learners
Deploy quick anonymous survey

stages.title: Stage 2: Choosing Your Design Approach
stages.description:
Your research reveals that different learners need different things: some need visual guides, others need simpler layouts, others need built-in instructions. You're deciding between design philosophies:

Option A: Design one "universal" interface that works well for everyone (£8,500). This requires careful thinking about every element. Takes longer but creates one unified experience.

Option B: Create multiple interface themes users can choose from (£11,000). Lets learners pick what works for them—classic minimalist, detailed with guides, simplified, etc. More flexible but more complex to maintain.

choices.text: Design single universal interface optimized for all users
Create multiple interface themes users can choose

stages.title: Stage 3: Assembling Design and UX Team
stages.description:
Your current design team is small and stretched. To pull off a redesign while keeping the platform running, you need:

Option A: Hire a freelance inclusive design specialist (£6,000) for 2 months to lead the redesign and mentor your existing team. They bring expertise in neurodiversity-friendly design and can train your team.

Option B: Send your existing designers to an online UX course (£1,500 total). They learn on the job but it's slower and might not cover everything you need.

choices.text: Hire freelance inclusive design specialist for intensive project
Train existing team with online courses

stages.title: Stage 4: Implementation and Testing
stages.description:
Now you're actually redesigning. The question is how much feedback you get during the process:

Option A: Release a beta version to a small group of 50 diverse learners (£2,000 for incentives and support), gather feedback for 2 weeks, then refine before full launch. Slower but validates your design works.

Option B: Redesign internally, then launch to everyone (£200 for basic testing). Faster but riskier if users still struggle.

choices.text: Beta test with diverse learner group before full launch
Design internally, launch to everyone

stages.title: Stage 5: Building Inclusive Design Into Your Culture
stages.description:
After the redesign launches, you need to ensure it doesn't regress and that future updates maintain inclusivity:

Option A: Hire a part-time UX accessibility auditor (£2,500/month) who reviews every new feature for inclusive design before it launches. Expensive but ensures consistency.

Option B: Add checklists to your development process (£300) that developers review themselves. Cheaper but less expert oversight.

choices.text: Hire UX accessibility auditor for ongoing review
Add self-review checklists to development process

stages.title: Stage 6: Extending Inclusive Design to Mobile
stages.description:
Your desktop redesign is successful, but 50% of your learners use mobile devices. Mobile interfaces need different considerations for inclusivity:

Option A: Redesign the mobile app from scratch with inclusive principles (£5,500). Touch targets are larger, navigation is simplified, and it works with mobile screen readers and voice commands.

Option B: Make minimal mobile improvements (£1,500). Responsive design works okay but doesn't leverage mobile accessibility features.

choices.text: Fully redesign mobile app for inclusive design
Make minimal responsive design improvements

stages.title: Stage 7: Scaling Beyond Your Platform
stages.description:
Your inclusive design work is getting noticed. Other educational platforms want to learn from you. You're considering:

Option A: Create and publish an open-source design system (£4,000) that other platforms can use. This shares your knowledge but doesn't directly benefit your platform and takes resources away from new features.

Option B: Keep your design system proprietary and focus on building new features (£800 for documentation). Protects your competitive advantage but misses opportunity to improve the entire industry.

choices.text: Publish open-source design system for industry adoption
Keep design system proprietary and focus internally

problems.title: The Algorithm Bias Scandal
problems.theme: Fairness and Bias in Tech Hiring Systems
problems.briefDescription:
Your company, TalentMatch AI, has built an intelligent hiring platform used by hundreds of companies across the UK to screen job applications. It uses machine learning to identify the best candidates quickly and reduce bias.

Then you discover something troubling: a data scientist on your team finds that your algorithm systematically ranks qualified women and people of color lower than white men who have identical qualifications. It's not intentional discrimination—it's statistical bias baked into your training data. But the damage is real: thousands of diverse candidates have been filtered out unfairly.

The discovery becomes public. Hiring discrimination advocates demand action. Companies using your platform are panicking. Your board is asking: how do you fix an algorithm that's already caused harm while rebuilding trust in your entire system?

Your decisions will affect how quickly you fix the bias problem (Impact), whether you truly make the hiring process fairer for all candidates (Inclusivity), whether companies and candidates trust your platform again (Trust), and what resources you have left to invest in long-term improvement (Budget in £).
problems.objectives: Address algorithmic bias in hiring. Make decisions that fix discrimination, rebuild trust, and prove your commitment to fairness.

stages.title: Stage 1: Public Response and Immediate Action
stages.description:
The bias discovery is headlines. You need to act fast. Do you:

Option A: Immediately pause the hiring system's AI scoring (£2,000), issue a detailed public apology with specific examples of bias found, and commit to publishing quarterly fairness audits. This is honest but might scare away customers temporarily.

Option B: Quickly patch the most obvious bias issues (£500), issue a brief statement saying you're "continuously improving," and keep most of the system running. Faster to market but feels like minimizing the problem.

choices.text: Pause system, detailed apology, commit to transparency
Quick patch, brief statement, keep system mostly running

stages.title: Stage 2: Assembling Diverse AI Ethics Team
stages.description:
You need experts who can audit and fix bias. Your options:

Option A: Hire external AI ethics consultants and hire 2 full-time diverse data scientists (£7,000 total). They bring specialized expertise in fairness and represent perspectives the bias may have missed.

Option B: Use your existing team and attend an online fairness course (£1,500). Cheaper but your homogeneous team may replicate similar blind spots.

choices.text: Hire external ethics consultants + diverse data scientists
Train existing team with online course

stages.title: Stage 3: Comprehensive Fairness Audit
stages.description:
You need to understand exactly how bad the bias is across all demographics. Options:

Option A: Commission an independent third-party audit (£8,000) that will test your algorithm against 50+ demographic combinations. Credible but slow (6 weeks) and findings could be damaging.

Option B: Do an internal audit using fairness metrics (£2,000). Faster but your findings might be questioned by clients and regulators.

choices.text: Commission independent third-party audit
Conduct internal fairness audit

stages.title: Stage 4: Rebuilding the Algorithm
stages.description:
Now you must rebuild the algorithm to remove bias. Two approaches:

Option A: Complete algorithm redesign (£12,000). Rebuild from scratch using fairness-first principles, test extensively with diverse candidates, train on debiased data. Takes 10 weeks but creates genuinely fair system.

Option B: Patch the existing algorithm (£4,000). Add fairness constraints and reweight the algorithm. Fast (2 weeks) but may not fully fix underlying issues.

choices.text: Completely rebuild algorithm with fairness-first design
Add fairness constraints to existing algorithm

stages.title: Stage 5: Community Engagement and Testing
stages.description:
You need to test with actual diverse candidates and hiring managers. How do you engage?

Option A: Form a hiring equity board (£3,500) with 12-15 people from underrepresented groups, hiring managers, and advocates. Test the algorithm and provide ongoing feedback.

Option B: Recruit volunteers online for testing (£500). Faster but less structured community partnership.

choices.text: Form hiring equity advisory board with vulnerable communities
Recruit online volunteers for testing

stages.title: Stage 6: Long-Term Fairness Governance
stages.description:
You need systems to prevent bias from creeping back in. What's your strategy?

Option A: Hire a Chief Fairness Officer (£3,200) and establish quarterly bias audits, mandatory bias training for all engineers, and a fairness review board. Shows serious commitment.

Option B: Add fairness checklists to development (£300). Teams review for bias themselves before launch.

choices.text: Hire Chief Fairness Officer with quarterly audits
Add fairness review checklists to development process

stages.title: Stage 7: Industry Leadership and Transparency
stages.description:
Your work is getting noticed. You're considering your role in the broader industry:

Option A: Publish your fairness methodology and datasets (£4,500) as open-source. Help other companies build fair hiring systems. Shares your competitive advantage but improves the entire industry.

Option B: Keep your fairness methods proprietary (£1,000). Protect your competitive edge while others struggle with bias.

choices.text: Publish open-source fairness methodology and datasets
Keep fairness methods proprietary

problems.title: The Digital Divide Crisis
problems.theme: Equitable Access to Remote Learning Technology
problems.briefDescription:
Your educational platform, EduConnect, serves schools across rural and urban UK. You've built amazing remote learning features that reduced the achievement gap during lockdowns.

Then you discover the gap is widening again: students in rural areas and low-income families simply don't have reliable broadband. Some have to tether to mobile phone hotspots. Others share a single family device among 3+ children. Your fancy features—HD video, real-time collaboration, interactive simulations—simply don't work for them.

Meanwhile, privileged students with gigabit fiber and personal laptops are thriving. Your platform has become a tool that increases inequality instead of reducing it.

Now you must choose: do you redesign for low-bandwidth access? Do you provide devices? Do you push back against the reality that true digital equity requires massive resources?

Your decisions will impact how broadly your platform works (Impact), whether all students can truly participate (Inclusivity), whether families trust that you care about their circumstances (Trust), and what resources remain for other improvements (Budget in £).
problems.objectives: Bridge the digital divide. Make decisions that ensure rural and low-income students can fully participate in remote learning.

stages.title: Stage 1: Understanding the Connectivity Crisis
stages.description:
You need data on who's excluded. How do you gather it?

Option A: Commission a comprehensive survey (£6,000) of 2,000 students and families across rural/urban and income levels, testing actual connectivity and device access. Deep understanding but takes 4 weeks.

Option B: Collect quick feedback via your app (£400). Fast and cheap but less representative sample.

choices.text: Commission comprehensive connectivity and access survey
Collect quick in-app feedback

stages.title: Stage 2: Low-Bandwidth Platform Optimization
stages.description:
Your platform must work on slow connections. Options:

Option A: Complete platform redesign for offline-first, low-bandwidth access (£9,000). All features work on 3G, content syncs when connection available, videos auto-compress, text-based alternatives for all media.

Option B: Optimize videos and reduce image quality (£2,500). Helps but doesn't fix core issues for very slow connections.

choices.text: Complete redesign for offline-first low-bandwidth
Optimize videos and reduce image quality

stages.title: Stage 3: Device Access Program
stages.description:
Many students don't have their own device. Your options:

Option A: Launch a device loan program (£15,000) providing refurbished tablets/laptops to 200 low-income students per year. Transformative impact but very expensive and logistically complex.

Option B: Partner with schools to provide devices during school hours only (£3,000). Helps but doesn't solve home access problem.

choices.text: Launch comprehensive device loan program
Partner with schools for in-school device access

stages.title: Stage 4: Teacher Training and Blended Learning
stages.description:
Teachers need to adapt their teaching for mixed connectivity. How do you support them?

Option A: Launch comprehensive instructor training program (£5,000). Teachers learn to deliver mixed synchronous/asynchronous lessons, offline-friendly activities, group work designs that don't require everyone online simultaneously.

Option B: Create simple teacher guides (£800). Less comprehensive support but cheaper.

choices.text: Launch comprehensive blended learning instructor training
Publish simple teacher guides

stages.title: Stage 5: Low-Bandwidth Content Library
stages.description:
Create content designed for low connectivity. Options:

Option A: Develop text-based and voice-only versions of all courses (£6,000). Audio files work on 2G, text works on dial-up, everything is small bandwidth. Genuinely inclusive.

Option B: Create optional text summaries of video content (£1,500). Helps but less comprehensive.

choices.text: Develop complete text and voice-only course versions
Create text summaries for key video content

stages.title: Stage 6: Mobile-First and SMS Support
stages.description:
Most excluded students access via mobile phone. What's your strategy?

Option A: Rebuild entire platform mobile-first, SMS for notifications/submissions (£7,000). Works on basic phones with 2G network. Genuinely reaches everyone.

Option B: Create a basic mobile app (£2,000). Better than responsive web but still requires data.

choices.text: Mobile-first redesign with SMS support for 2G
Build basic mobile app

stages.title: Stage 7: Advocating for Broadband Infrastructure
stages.description:
Long-term solution requires better infrastructure. Your role:

Option A: Partner with government and NGOs on broadband advocacy (£3,500). Fund policy research, write policy briefs, present to parliament on digital equity. Shape industry standards and policy.

Option B: Focus internally on your platform (£500). Keep your head down and optimize what you control.

choices.text: Partner on broadband infrastructure advocacy
Stay focused on platform optimization

problems.title: The Cyberbullying Crisis
problems.theme: Student Safety and Responsible Content Moderation
problems.briefDescription:
Your social learning platform, ConnectEd, is wildly popular with teenagers. It lets students collaborate on projects, share learning resources, and connect across schools. You're proud of building something young people actually want to use.

Then you receive reports of cyberbullying, hate speech, and sexual harassment on your platform. Some students have attempted suicide after campaigns of harassment. Parents are demanding accountability. Schools are threatening to ban your platform. Mental health advocates say you're putting teen safety at risk by not moderating content aggressively enough.

You face an impossible dilemma: moderate too strictly and you kill the authentic community that makes the platform special. Moderate too loosely and you're allowing serious harm. Free speech advocates say any moderation is censorship. Child safety advocates say you're not doing enough.

Your decisions will impact how safe the platform actually is (Impact), whether all students can participate without fear (Inclusivity), whether families and schools trust you to protect young people (Trust), and what resources you have to invest in the moderation infrastructure (Budget in £).
problems.objectives: Protect student safety. Make decisions that prevent cyberbullying and harassment while preserving authentic community.

stages.title: Stage 1: Crisis Response and Immediate Safety
stages.description:
Young people are actively being harassed. Your immediate response:

Option A: Implement aggressive temporary content filters (£4,000), hire emergency moderators, create crisis support resources. Remove any post with reported harassment within 1 hour. Partner with mental health organizations. This prevents immediate harm but may feel heavy-handed.

Option B: Improve reporting tools and train community moderators (£1,000). Let users flag content, empower peer moderation. Faster to implement but slower response to harm.

choices.text: Aggressive filtering and emergency moderation team
Enhanced reporting and peer moderation

stages.title: Stage 2: Building Safety Expertise
stages.description:
You need genuine safety experts, not just engineers. Options:

Option A: Hire a VP of Safety with expertise in teen safety, content moderation, and mental health (£5,000). Build a team that understands the nuance—not overreach, not under-protect.

Option B: Hire a moderator coordinator (£2,000). Manage moderation but less strategic expertise.

choices.text: Hire VP of Safety with specialist expertise
Hire moderator coordinator

stages.title: Stage 3: Moderation Infrastructure Policy
stages.description:
You need fair, transparent moderation policies. Options:

Option A: Build comprehensive moderation policy (£6,000) with clear definitions, appeal processes, transparency reports, and human review for edge cases. Slow but fair and defensible.

Option B: Use automated filters (£2,000). Fast but risks both false positives (censoring harmless content) and false negatives (missing real harm).

choices.text: Build comprehensive policy with human review
Implement automated content filters

stages.title: Stage 4: Mental Health Support Infrastructure
stages.description:
Moderation alone won't solve the problem. You need support for affected students:

Option A: Partner with mental health services (£4,500). Partner with organizations that can provide counseling, crisis hotline access, and support to harassed students. Genuine help but not your core competency.

Option B: Create in-app support resources (£800). Links to crisis hotlines, self-help guides. Less comprehensive.

choices.text: Partner with mental health services for direct support
Create in-app support resources and guides

stages.title: Stage 5: Digital Citizenship and Parent Education
stages.description:
Prevention is better than moderation. Options:

Option A: Create comprehensive digital citizenship curriculum (£5,000) for students and parent training program. Teach empathy, consequences of harassment, media literacy, how to be an upstander rather than bystander.

Option B: Create simple guides (£1,000). Cheaper but less transformative.

choices.text: Develop digital citizenship curriculum and parent training
Create simple guides

stages.title: Stage 6: Transparency and Accountability
stages.description:
How transparent are you about what happens on your platform?

Option A: Publish detailed transparency reports (£3,000) quarterly showing harassment reports, actions taken, appeal outcomes, demographic breakdowns. Shows you're serious about accountability.

Option B: Keep moderation details private (£300). Protects harassed students' privacy but raises trust questions.

choices.text: Publish detailed quarterly transparency reports
Keep moderation details confidential

stages.title: Stage 7: Community Safety Board
stages.description:
Who decides what's acceptable on your platform?

Option A: Establish Community Safety Board (£2,500) with student representatives, parents, educators, mental health experts. They review policies, hear appeals, guide platform decisions. Genuinely democratic.

OptionB: Focus on expert-led safety (£500). Your team makes decisions without community input.

choices.text: Establish Community Safety Board with diverse stakeholders
Expert-led safety decisions

stages.title: The Server Outage Disaster
stages.theme: Reliability and Incident Response in Cloud Services
stages.briefDescription:
Your SaaS education platform, CloudClass, runs on a cluster of servers. One night a datacenter power failure causes a full outage, leaving thousands of students and teachers locked out of the system during exam week. Schools are furious, regulators are demanding answers, and your CTO is getting calls from investors.

You must make decisions under intense pressure: restore service quickly, communicate honestly, and decide how much to invest in redundancy versus cost savings. Every choice affects uptime (Impact), customer trust (Trust), user inclusivity (Inclusivity) since some areas have no alternative systems, and your operational budget (Budget in £).
stages.objectives: Recover from a catastrophic outage while rebuilding faith in your platform and deciding how much to spend on future reliability.

stages.title: Stage 1: Immediate Response
stages.description:
The platform is down and complaints are pouring in. You can:

Option A: Failover to a backup datacenter (£5,000) even though it's slow to spin up, and send a frank apology email explaining the outage. This regains some trust but costs money.

Option B: Try to patch the primary servers quickly (£1,000), send a vague "technical difficulties" notice, and hope customers wait. Saves cash but risks bigger blowback if it fails again.

choices.text: Failover to backup and send honest apology
Patch primary and send vague notice

stages.title: Stage 2: Root Cause Investigation
stages.description:
Once service is restored, you must find out what went wrong. You can:

Option A: Hire external consultants to audit your infrastructure (£6,000). Takes time but gives credibility.

Option B: Let your in‑house team dig through logs (£1,500). Faster but may miss hidden issues.

choices.text: External infrastructure audit
Internal log review

stages.title: Stage 3: Customer Communication
stages.description:
How transparent will you be moving forward?

Option A: Publish full post-mortem with timeline and fixes (£2,000). Builds trust but reveals weakness.

Option B: Share only high-level summary (£500). Safer but may leave customers skeptical.

choices.text: Publish full post-mortem
Share high-level summary

stages.title: Stage 4: Investing in Redundancy
stages.description:
Do you spend to prevent future outages?

Option A: Build multi-region redundancy (£10,000). Expensive but nearly eliminates single-point failures.

Option B: Add local UPS and backups (£3,000). Less costly but still vulnerable to datacenter-level events.

choices.text: Multi-region redundancy
Local UPS and backups

stages.title: Stage 5: Disaster Recovery Drills
stages.description:
You need to practice so staff aren't caught off guard. Options:

Option A: Run quarterly full drills with downtime simulate (£4,000). Disruptive but ensures preparedness.

Option B: Do annual tabletop exercises (£800). Minimal disruption but less realistic.

choices.text: Quarterly full disaster drills
Annual tabletop exercises

stages.title: Stage 6: Pricing for Reliability
stages.description:
Should you charge more for higher uptime?

Option A: Introduce premium plans with uptime guarantees (£5,000 development cost). Extra revenue but may alienate budget customers.

Option B: Keep flat pricing and absorb costs (£0 change). Simpler but puts burden on company.

choices.text: Premium uptime plans
Flat pricing, absorb costs

stages.title: Stage 7: Rebuilding Brand Image
stages.description:
After the drama, how do you regain market share?

Option A: Launch a marketing campaign highlighting new reliability features (£6,000). Risky but can turn crisis into selling point.

Option B: Rely on word-of-mouth from existing satisfied customers (£1,000). Cheaper but slower recovery.

choices.text: Marketing campaign for reliability
Word-of-mouth recovery

stages.title: The AI Surveillance Dilemma
stages.theme: Privacy vs. Safety in School Monitoring Tools
stages.briefDescription:
Your company supplies an AI-powered monitoring tool used by schools to detect potential threats and improve student wellbeing by analyzing chat logs and video feeds. After installation, parents discover the system also flags benign behaviour like wearing hoodies or speaking another language, leading to false reports and privacy outcry. Some students feel constantly watched and creativity is stifled.

You're torn between improving safety and respecting student privacy. Each choice affects perceived impact on wellbeing, inclusivity of diverse students, trust from parents and regulators, and your development budget.
stages.objectives: Balance safety with privacy. Decide how invasive the monitoring should be and how transparent you are about data use.

stages.title: Stage 1: Establish Monitoring Policy
stages.description:
Do you:

Option A: Define strict privacy rules and limit monitoring to explicit threats (£3,000). May miss subtle issues but respects privacy.

Option B: Monitor broad behaviour patterns (£1,000). Catch more potential problems but raise privacy concerns.

choices.text: Strict privacy-first monitoring
Broad behaviour monitoring

stages.title: Stage 2: Transparency to Stakeholders
stages.description:
How open are you?

Option A: Publish all data practices and allow opt-outs (£2,000). Builds trust but may reduce usefulness.

Option B: Keep algorithms proprietary (£500). Faster innovation but invites suspicion.

choices.text: Full transparency with opt-outs
Proprietary algorithms

stages.title: Stage 3: Improving Detection Accuracy
stages.description:
Invest in better models?

Option A: Hire specialty data scientists (£7,000) to reduce false positives. Expensive but reduces harm.

Option B: Use off-the-shelf models (£2,000). Cheaper but less accurate.

choices.text: Specialty data scientists
Off-the-shelf models

stages.title: Stage 4: Oversight and Appeals
stages.description:
Who reviews flagged cases?

Option A: Create independent oversight board with parents and ethics experts (£3,500). Adds credibility but slows response.

Option B: Keep review internal (£1,000). Faster but less trusted.

choices.text: Independent oversight board
Internal review only

stages.title: Stage 5: Teacher and Staff Training
stages.description:
Decide how much training to provide to interpret alerts.

Option A: Extensive training program (£4,000). Reduces misuse but costs time.

Option B: Basic quick-start guides (£800). Faster but risks misinterpretation.

choices.text: Extensive training program
Basic quick-start guides

stages.title: Stage 6: Data Retention and Privacy
stages.description:
How long do you keep data?

Option A: Retain minimal data for 30 days (£2,500). Limits privacy risk.

Option B: Keep data indefinitely for research (£1,000). Useful but privacy-invasive.

choices.text: Retain minimal 30-day data
Indefinite data retention

stages.title: Stage 7: Community Feedback Mechanism
stages.description:
How do users influence the product?

Option A: Build robust feedback portal with dashboards (£3,000). Encourages engagement but resource-heavy.

Option B: Use periodic surveys (£600). Cheaper but less interactive.

choices.text: Robust feedback portal
Periodic surveys
